coredata.dc:description: A luminescence-based method was developed to detect gentamicin using silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) associated with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). When gentamicin sulfate interacts with the AgNPs/N-GQDs system, the characteristic blue fluorescence of N-GQDs, which had been previously turned off by AgNPs, is restored. Under specific conditions (such as the amount of synthesis dispersion and pH), this AgNPs/N-GQDs probe enabled quantification of gentamicin ranging from 3.0 × 10−7 to 6.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. To address interference from other substances during the analysis, a solid-phase extraction, with a kanamycin-imprinted polymer cartridge, enabled accurate results. Two veterinary pharmaceutical samples were used to test the method and results were in agreement with those achieved by obtained using high-performance liquid chromatography with analyte chemical derivatization. This new method is straightforward, sensitive, and selective, and it is also considered eco-friendly (0.63 score Analytical greenness calculator) since it avoids the use of toxic chemical derivatization reagents, use nanoquantities of carbon and silver based nanomaterials and aqueous systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A luminescence-based method was developed to detect gentamicin using silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) associated with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). When gentamicin sulfate interacts with the AgNPs/N-GQDs system, the characteristic blue fluorescence of N-GQDs, which had been previously turned off by AgNPs, is restored. Under specific conditions (such as the amount of synthesis dispersion and pH), this AgNPs/N-GQDs probe enabled quantification of gentamicin ranging from 3.0 × 10−7 to 6.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. To address interference from other substances during the analysis, a solid-phase extraction, with a kanamycin-imprinted polymer cartridge, enabled accurate results. Two veterinary pharmaceutical samples were used to test the method and results were in agreement with those achieved by obtained using high-performance liquid chromatography with analyte chemical derivatization. This new method is straightforward, sensitive, and selective, and it is also considered eco-friendly (0.63 score Analytical greenness calculator) since it avoids the use of toxic chemical derivatization reagents, use nanoquantities of carbon and silver based nanomaterials and aqueous systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Gentamycin is used to treat hospital-acquired infections in humans [1,2] and is also frequently used in veterinary medicine [3]. Gentamycin comprises three main species differing on how the 2-amino-hexose ring is methylated. While gentamycin C1a presents no methyl groups, gentamycins C1 and C2 are methylated at the 6′ position, with C1 being N-methylated (secondary amine) and C1a and C2 presenting primary amine groups. Additionally, gentamycin C2 is comprised by a pair of stereoisomers [4]. The commercial form of gentamycin is a sulfate of a mixture of these species [5].

High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is utilized to determine gentamycin with fluorimetric detection using chemical derivatization (o-phthalaldehyde) achieving limit of detection (LOD) of 2.1 × 10⁻⁶ mol L⁻¹ [4]. Amperometric detection has been utilized in HPLC analysis, reaching LOD of 2.1 × 10⁻⁷ mol L⁻¹ [6]. Gentamicin has been determined by spectrofluorimetry after chemical derivatization, with 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole, enabling LOD of 1.6 × 10⁻⁷ mol L⁻¹ but with a restricted dynamic linear range [7].

The use of metal nanoparticles and carbon nanomaterials have been extensively evaluated in various fields as they present unique physical and chemical properties, catalytic activity, and chemical stability [8,9]. Carbon nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered attention due to their optical properties [10–13] prone to changes as they interact with organic molecules [14–16]. This characteristic has been explored in photoluminescence analytical sensing, often achieving selectivity as GQDs interact with chemical species in aqueous systems, with an efficiency that depends upon different factors such as pH and nanoparticle surface functionalization [11,17].

GQDs combined with spherical gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been proposed as sensor to determine kanamycin. Specifically, the original photoluminescence from the GQDs, previously quenched after combined with AuNPs, was restored in the presence of kanamycin [18,19]. Shi et al. [20] described a nanosensor based on colorimetric and photoluminometic properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and AuNPs to determine glutathione. CQDs served as photoluminescent indicator while AuNPs produced the colorimetric response also serving as a quencher of CQDs luminescence. Increasing glutathione concentrations affect AuNPs localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and recovered photoluminescence from CQDs. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) in aqueous dispersions have been employed to quantify gentamicin, as the nanoparticle LSPR band is gradually affected by the increasing concentrations of gentamicin [2]. Ahmed et al. [21] produced CQDs by boiling an alkaline pectin solution with the resulting dispersion used to detect Ag+ as it is reduced into AgNPs, producing an LSPR band as the measurable response. The association of AgNPs and CQDs in aqueous dispersion was used for the sensing of melamine with a dual-mode response, one based on the decreasing of the AgNPs LSPR band and the other due to the CQDs luminescence restoration as the concentration of melamine increased in the system [22]. Another study involved combination of AgNPs and CQDs for colorimetric sensing of Cu2+ [23].

A research investigated how gentamicin interacts with AuNPs (spherical and nanorods) in aqueous dispersion then using AuNPs to indirectly detect the antibiotic [24]. The method did not require toxic reagents for derivatization with no significant interference observed from other substances present in the veterinary pharmaceuticals analyzed [24]. AuNPs have been used as colorimetric probes to detect aminoglycosides [24,25] due to the LSPR perturbation observed when they interact with AuNPs [26,27].

Another relevant materials of analytical interest is molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), synthesized by polymerization in the presence of a template molecule that creates sites, after template removal, complementary to a target compound structure. MIPs are advantageous due to their simple, cost-effective production, chemical stability under extreme conditions, and selective affinity for analytes with structures similar to the template. These materials have been applied in liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, in solid-phase separations as sensors and in catalysis [28,29].

The present work involved creating a hybrid nanomaterial of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and AgNPs to detect gentamycin which is a molecule that present no relevant absorption in the UV–visible, do not fluoresce and has no significant redox reactions within potential windows of common electrodes. A study was made focusing on how the photoluminescence of N-GQDs is affected by AgNPs of different sizes, and how AgNPs modulates analytical response towards gentamycin, enabling a method to analyze veterinary pharmaceutical samples. Sample solutions underwent a solid-phase extraction (SPE) procedure using an aminoglycoside-selective MIP before being added into the N-GQDs/AgNPs dispersion for photoluminescence measurements. The results agreed with those obtained using HPLC after chemical derivatization of gentamycin. This method requires no toxic chemical derivatization procedures, simplifying the process and making it more environmentally friendly. This was indicated by the green profile obtained using the AGREE calculator [30].

Extinction/absorption was measured using a Perkin-Elmer (Walthman, MA, USA) Lambda 35 spectrophotometer with samples placed into 10 nm optical path quartz cuvette. Photoluminescence was acquired using an LS 55 luminescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer). Temperature effects on luminescence were studied using a PTP-1 stirred thermostatic system with a PCB1500 water-controlled fluid circulator (Perkin-Elmer). Images were acquired with a JEOL (model JSM-6701F, Tokyo, Japan) field emission scanning electron microscope in scanning transmission mode (STEM) at 30 kV. Raman spectra were obtained using an NT-MDT micro-Raman spectrometer (model NTegra spectra, Moscow, Russia). A carbon analyzer model TOC-VCPN (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) was employed in determining total carbon content. The hydrodynamic radius and zeta potential measurements were conducted with a model SZ-100 nanoparticle analyzer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan). Chromatography was performed on a high-performance liquid chromatograph (Agilent Technologies, Tokyo, Japan) model 1200 with a fluorescence detector. Solid phase extraction was assisted by a flow injection analysis (FIA) system (FIAlab 2000, Fialab Instruments, Seattle, WA, USA). The pH measurements were obtained using a Technopon pH meter (São Paulo, Brazil) with a combined glass membrane and Ag|AgCl(s)(KCl(sat)) electrodes.

The Milli-Q gradient A10 ultra-purifier (Millipore, Burlington, MA, USA) provided the ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm). Gentamycin sulfate, kanamycin sulfate, triethylamine, sodium metabisulfite, reduced L-glutathione (GSH), anhydrous disodium EDTA, methylparaben, propylparaben, 2-mercaptoethanol, (3-aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS), sodium borohydride, silver nitrate, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, MO, USA). The reagents o-phthalaldehyde (OPA), ammonium acetate, sodium hydroxide, methanol (HPLC-grade), acetonitrile (HPLC-grade), boric acid, hydrochloric acid, and citric acid were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Syringe filters, with a pore size of 0.45 μm, were obtained from Whatman, Kent, UK. Dialysis membranes (retaining molecular weight of 3.5 kDa) were acquired from Spectrum Laboratories Inc. (Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped quantum dots (N-GQDs) were created by hydro-exfoliating a melted and partially carbonized blend of 1.0 g of citric acid and 0.3 g of glutathione (nitrogen source) using a procedure described in a previous study [31]. The mixture of precursors was placed into a beaker and heated at 250 °C on a hot plate. As the mixture turned light brown (around 5 min) it was mixed with 100 mL of ultrapure water, leading to a yellowish mixture. Subsequently, this solution underwent a 24 h dialysis process to achieve an aqueous dispersion of N-GQDs.

Characterization of N-GQDs, in dispersion after dialysis, was made as reported by Pinto et al. [29]. Dialyzed dispersion presented a total carbon concentration (TCC) of 132 mg L−1. The dispersion showed an extinction spectrum with a maximum at 220 nm with a second band peaking at 345 nm (corresponding to the first excitonic). Photoluminescence emission presents maximum at 435 nm (blue color) under excitation at 345 nm. As gentamycin presents neither absorbance in the UV–visible nor photoluminescence (when excited at 345 nm), it can be concluded that the inner-filter effect caused by the aminoglycoside is unlikely to occur.

As reported by Pinto et al. [29], studies carried out by XPS (in an N-GQDs dispersion pre-concentrated on support) showed N in the structure of GQDs, which is probably found in the form of amino groups at the edges of graphene nanoflakes. Analysis revealed a peak at a binding energy of 399.5 eV indicating that N is primarily bound to C (C-N of amines). A second peak at 401.0 eV is related to graphite-bound nitrogen. Scanning electron microscopic analysis coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray (SEM-EDX) was conducted to obtain its elemental composition: % C = 51.9, % O = 42.9, % N = 2.9, and % S = 2.6. The presence of sulfur is attributed to use of glutathione as precursor.

Preparation of the AgNPs dispersion involves the chemical reduction of AgNO3, in solution, using NaBH4 [32]. An aqueous solution of NaBH4 (2.4 × 10−3 mol L−1) was placed in a reaction flask and continuously stirred while immersed in a cold water bath. Then, the AgNO3 solution (1.2 × 10−3 mol L−1) was added (about one drop per second) resulting in a clean dark yellow mixture. After stirring for 2 min, water was added (to complete 10 mL) before the mixture was stored in amber flasks under refrigeration. The volumes of precursors solutions were varied in different syntheses using AgNO3:NaBH4 molar ratios of: 1:20, 1:10, 1:4, and 1:2. The formation of AgNPs was confirmed by measuring the typical LSPR band using transmittance spectrophotometry (UV–vis). To preserve the nanoparticle dispersions, they were refrigerated and shielded from light and the AgNPs dispersions were equilibrated to room temperature before use.

AgNPs/N-GQDs systems were prepared by combining, under vigorous stirring, different concentrations of the AgNPs dispersions (with different nanoparticle diameters obtained from the syntheses of AgNPs (using different AgNO3:NaBH4 proportions) with aqueous dispersion of N-GQDs, at different TCC adjusted by dilution, with water, of the original dialyzed N-GQDs dispersion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP)
ce:section.ce:para: Solid-phase extraction (SPE) was performed to improve selectivity in gentamycin determination by using a MIP imprinted for aminoglycosides (MIP-AMG) prepared using the procedure previously reported [33], with kanamycin sulfate as the aminoglycoside template. Kanamycin was chosen as it was found to be suitable to obtain an aminoglycoside selective polymer, because it not hygroscopic facilitating manipulation and also due the easiness to remove it from the polymer during preparation [19,33]. The process involved dissolving 500 mg of the kanamycin sulfate in 6 mL water following addition of 3.2 mL of APTMS, 2.7 mL of TEOS, and 0.4 mL of a 1.0 mol L⁻¹ HCl solution. This mixture was heated to 40 °C under stirring and the produced gel was dried, for 48 h, at room temperature. The dried solid material was macerated and washed with 3 aliquots of water (total volume of 1.00 L) followed by 3 aliquots of methanol (total volume of 300 mL) to remove the template. The MIP was manually macerated in an agate mortar and the pulverized material was sieved to select size distribution between 106 and 150 µm. In a prior investigation by Pinto et al., the polymer was characterized using SEM-EDX, identifying Si (31.0 %), O (44.3 %) and C (24.8 %) [29].

Stock solutions of gentamycin were prepared using their corresponding sulfates dissolved in water to obtain concentrations of 5.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 and 5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1. The AgNPs/N-GQDs-amino probe (used for determinations) was prepared by mixing the 3.0 mL of the N-GQDs working dispersion with 4.0 mL of the AgNPs working dispersion, adjusting final volume to 10.0 mL. To prepare the N-GQDs working dispersion, the N-GQDs original dialyzed dispersion was diluted ten times with deionized water, and then 240 µL of the resulting diluted dispersion was transferred to a 10 mL volumetric flask to adjust final volume with water. To prepare the working dispersion of AgNPs, 30 mL of the original AgNPs dispersion was mixed with 10.0 mL of water, resulting in an AgNPs concentration estimated at 8.0 × 10−10 mol L−1 (based on an average nanoparticle diameter of 20 nm).

For the HPLC analyses, a gentamycin sulfate stock solution (1000 mg L−1) was prepared, and the analytical curve was made by diluting this solution with water. The analyte standards were prepared between 25 and 250 µg L−1. For chemical derivatization, OPA (0.05 g) was dissolved in 2.00 mL of methanol, followed by the addition of 0.04 mL of 2-mercaptoethanol. The volume of solution was then adjusted to 10.0 mL using a 0.40 mol L⁻¹ borate buffer solution (pH 9.5). Analyzes were performed using a mixture of 400 µL of the sample solution (or standard) with 150 µL the derivatizing solution, which was left to react during 10 min before use.

Nanomaterials were evaluated by scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM). Five µL of the aqueous dispersions of N-GQDs, AgNPs, and AgNPs/N-GQDs were deposited onto carbon grids and air-dried, at room temperature.

Photoluminescence spectra were recorded with a 10.0 nm spectral band passes (excitation/emission) and scan rate of 1000 nm min−1. Quantitative measurements were made using a fixed volume of an AgNO3/N-GQDs dispersion placed in the volumetric flasks. Suitable volumes of gentamycin standard solution (or a diluted sample) were added before the final volumes of solution (10.0 mL) were adjusted with water. Steady-state measurements were conducted using quartz cuvettes at 345/455 nm (excitation/emission). Measurements of the blanks were carried out on the nanomaterial dispersions without the addition of analyte. Signals were mathematically treated to determine intensity difference before (L0) and after the analyte addition (L). This value was normalized using L0, thus establishing a normalized signal, in this case (L − L0)/L0, in function of gentamycin concentration. Absorption/extinction spectra were obtained from solutions in quartz cuvettes by scanning from 200 to 700 nm at a 1000 nm min−1 rate and using a spectral bandpass of 10 nm. A solution at 5.00 × 10−4 mol L−1 was used to obtain the typical absorption spectrum from gentamycin. For the AgNPs, 3.00 mL of the AgNP synthesis dispersion was diluted with water (10.0 mL final volume). For the AgNPs/N-GQDs dispersions, a mixture of 4.0 mL of the N-GQDs working dispersion and 3.0 mL of the AgNP working dispersion was used. The final volume was adjusted to 10.0 mL with water. Micro-volume samples or analyte standards were added before final volume adjusted with water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation and solid-phase extraction and chromatographic analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Two gentamycin-based veterinary pharmaceutical formulations, designated as A and B, were analyzed after dilution with water. The main component of both formulations is gentamycin sulfate (44.0 mg mL−1 or 9.2 × 10−2 mol L−1) in formulation A and in formulation B (40.0 mg mL−1 or 8.3 × 10−2 mol L−1). Additionally, the formulations contained sodium metabisulfite, EDTA, methylparaben, and propylparaben.

SPE employed the MIP-AMG to enhance accuracy in quantifying gentamycin. The SPE cartridges were constructed packing 100 mg of the MIP-AMG in a 3.0 mL plastic syringes. For the extraction process, the sample was loaded, allowing the interaction of the aminoglycoside with the polymer for 1 h. Subsequently, cleaning to eliminate excipients was carried out with aliquots of 1.0 mL of water [33] then, gentamycin was eluted with 1.0 mL aqueous acidic solution (pH 3.5 adjusted with 0.01 mol L−1 HCl). This entire procedure was semi-automated with the assistance of a FIA system at a flow rate set at 1 mL min−1. The HPLC determination was based on the use of OPA for chemically modifying the analyte, following the method reported by Zhou et al. [34].

Different AgNPs were tested to create dispersion systems of AgNPs/N-GQDs. The molar ratios of precursors were adjusted by mixing varied volumes of an AgNO3 solution with a NaBH4 solution. The molar ratios between AgNO3 and NaBH4 were: i) 1:20, ii) 1:10, iii) 1:4, and iv) 1:2. The LSPR band maximum for each AgNP dispersions is in Table 1. When the ratio of chemical precursors was adjusted from i to iv, the size of AgNPs decreased (LSPR maximum shifting to blue), indicating production of more nanoparticle seeds, which hindered nanoparticle growth. The typical hydrodynamic diameter distribution is shown in Fig. 1A (obtained for AgNPs with average diameter of 20.0 ± 4.3 nm).

For each synthesis, the AgNPs concentration was estimated [35] assuming the sphericity of AgNPs and that the number of Ag atoms in each nanoparticle (N) equals 3.1 × 1021 multiplied by the cube of the nanoparticle diameter in nm (d3). By calculating the value of N, the concentration of AgNPs in the dispersion was obtained through Eq. 1, where NTotal is number of Ag atoms in the reaction solution, V is the volume of the aqueous mixture (dm3), and NA represents Avogadro's constant.
(1)
C =
N

A

The dispersion with the smaller nanoparticle size resulted in higher concentration of AgNPs due to the formation of more seeds and greater quantity of the silver precursor available. The spectral profiles of AgNPs with average hydrodynamic diameter of 20 nm are illustrated in Fig. 1B, with the integrated area revealing that the LSPR intensity is proportional to the number of nanoparticles present. The estimated AgNPs concentrations varied from 8.0 × 10−11 to 3.0 × 10−9 mol L−1 as average hydrodynamic diameter (obtained by DLS) varied respectively from 36 nm to 15 nm (see Table 1).

Based on the preliminary studies, the conditions that produced the most stable and uniform AgNPs were selected for further use in the combination with N-GQDs. A summary of the optimized parameters, including precursor concentrations, reaction conditions, and the observed properties of the synthesized nanoparticles are in Table 2. The selection of these conditions was guided by their ability to produce AgNPs with consistent hydrodynamic diameters, strong colloidal stability, and a well-defined LSPR band.

Based on the FE-STEM images, the average diameter of AgNPs with hydrodynamic diameter of 20 nm was, in fact, 11.9 ± 3.9 nm (as shown in Fig. 2A). Images were computationally processed to determine circularity (0.80 ± 0.10, as shown in Fig. 2B) and the aspect ratio (1.20 ± 0.20, as shown in Fig. 2C). For N-GQDs, the obtained dispersion presented size distribution ranging from 10 to 40 nm, with average diameter of 17.9 ± 7.6 nm, as shown in the STEM image and size histogram ( Fig. 3).

To create the N-GQDs working dispersion, 240 μL of a 100-fold diluted N-GQDs dispersion was taken and further diluted to final volume of 10.0 mL. As a result, the pH of the N-GQDs dispersion, originally at pH 4.0, increased to pH 6.5. Before mixing, the concentration of the AgNPs original dispersion was decreased to ensure that the LSPR band maximum intensity was about 1.0. This was accomplished by mixing 10.0 mL of water with a 30.0 mL AgNPs original dispersion, forming the "AgNPs working dispersion." For the initial experiments involving the mixing of nanoparticles, it was mixed 6.0 mL of the N-GQDs working dispersion with 2.0 mL or 3.0 mL of the AgNPs probe dispersion before adjusting, with water, the final volume (10.0 mL).

Mixing AgNPs produced partial decreasing of the original photoluminescence emitted by N-GQDs (under excitation at 345 nm). The extent of the observed quenching was influenced by both the size and the quantity of the AgNPs but it was not necessarily proportional to the changing in the fraction of AgNO3 used in the mixture: For AgNPs with the hydrodynamic average of 36 nm, the 2.0 mL of AgNPs dispersion resulted in luminescence quenching of about 23 % (considering the original photoluminescence emitted in the absence of AgNPs but submitted to a similar dilution with only water). In contrast, when the AgNPs probe dispersion fraction was increased to 3.00 mL, further photoluminescence decreasing was only around 5 %, resulting in a total quenching of about 28 %. A similar trend was observed for AgNPs with hydrodynamic size of 28 nm.

N-GQDs photoluminescence quenching of about 56 % and 61 %, occurred in systems containing respectively 2.0 mL and 3.0 mL of the AgNPs dispersion with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 20 nm. This suggests that the limited coordination between N-GQDs and AgNPs may contribute to the observed results, as the suppression was similar despite a 50 % increase in AgNPs quantity.

When comparing the addition of the same volume of the AgNPs dispersion, it was found that nanoparticles of 20 nm average size produced the most effective quenching in the N-GQDs photoluminescence (creating a turn-off effect). This was the case even though the dispersion containing AgNPs with an average size of 15 nm had a higher concentration of metallic nanoparticles. These findings suggest that the effectiveness of quenching, which involves the improved interaction between N-GQDs and AgNPs, depends upon size and quantity of nanoparticles. The experiment also showed a shift towards longer wavelengths in the photoluminescence spectrum as the size and number of AgNPs of the same size increased. For example, when using AgNPs with an average size of 20 nm, the observed shifts in the photoluminescence spectrum were 10 nm and 15 nm for systems prepared respectively with 2.0 mL and 3.0 mL of the AgNPs working dispersion. This phenomenon indicates energy transfer between AgNPs and N-GQDs within the nanoparticle system. This energy transfer may decrease the energy bandgap of electronic transition, leading to spectral redshift. Alternatively, the result may indicate that a certain size fraction of the N-GQDs interacts preferentially with AgNPs, causing photoluminescence quenching of that fraction of the N-GQDs size population thus distorting spectrum.

When using nanoparticles with a 15 nm diameter and varying the quantity of AgNPs (by using either 2.0 or 3.0 mL of the working dispersion), the absence of any spectral shift alongside quenching may imply in less effective energy transfer affecting bandgap of N-GQDs.

The ζ-potential measurements indicated that the N-GQDs working dispersion (pH about 6.5 after dilution) had a surface charge of −(63.7 ± 3.1) mV, while the AgNPs working dispersion with an average nanoparticle size of 20 nm (original pH 7.8) had a surface charge of −(25.8 ± 2.0) mV. When combining the two to produce the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe (mixture using 3.0 mL of the AgNPs working dispersion), the resulting ζ-potential value was found to be of an intermediate value, in this case −(53.2 ± 0.7) mV. Since both types of nanoparticles carry a negative charge, it is improbable that an electrostatic interaction would take place between these two distinct nanoparticles. Nevertheless, due to silver's high chemical compatibility with nitrogen or sulfur (as a result of thiol functionalization created by using glutathione during the preparation of N-GQDs), it is suggested that a chemical reaction drives the formation of AgNPs/N-GQDs system.

The LSPR of AgNPs was also evaluated when associated with N-GQDs. Two dispersions were prepared for each one of different AgNP size: in one set, a simple dilution of the AgNPs dispersion with water was made, while for the other set, a dispersion of N-GQDs was added before the final dilution with water, keeping the concentration of AgNPs (of each size) similar to ensure comparisons. The position of the LSPR spectrum maxima was not affected by the presence of N-GQDs, probably because the size of the metallic nanoparticle core remained unchanged despite presence of N-GQDs. However, a slight decrease in the LSPR intensity was observed in all cases, indicating that N-GQDs slightly disturbed resonance among Ag atoms that form nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gentamycin effect on the optical properties of AgNPs/N-GQDs and AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments were conducted to test the use of AgNPs/N-GQDs as analytical gentamycin probe. Defined amounts of gentamicin sulfate were mixed with dispersions containing either AgNPs or AgNPs/N-GQDs to achieve final analyte concentrations ranging from 0.25 to 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. Notably, gentamycin does not affect the photoluminescence produced in aqueous dispersions of N-GQDs alone, but results presented in Fig. 4 show that the presence of gentamicin led to an enhancement in the photoluminescence from the dispersion of AgNPs/N-GQDs. However, the amount of gentamycin needed to produce this effect depended upon the of AgNPs.

Photoluminescence enhancement caused by gentamycin was less effective for AgNPs/N-GQDs systems prepared with larger AgNPs (hydrodynamic diameters of 28 and 36 nm), being only noticeable once gentamycin concentration surpassed 3.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. In these systems, the maximum signal enhancement induced by the analyte was only around 6. Conversely, for systems with smaller AgNPs (hydrodynamic diameters of 20 nm and 15 nm), the photoluminescence was enhanced by 27 and 23 %, respectively. Furthermore, comparing these two systems, better proportionality in signal increase (in the function of analyte concentration) was attained with systems containing AgNPs of 20 nm.

For the AgNPs/N-GQDs systems prepared with AgNPs of 20 nm, photoluminescence recovery exhibited spectral shifting; this was not observed in the system that contained AgNPs of 15 nm (Fig. S1 and Fig. 4). This agrees with trend observed for the quenching produced when AgNPs were combined with N-GQDs, producing red shifting when using AgNPs of 20 nm and quenching without spectral shifting when using AgNPs of 15 nm.

The ζ-potentials measurements of the AgNPs/N-GQDs system, prepared with AgNPs of 20 nm hydrodynamic diameter, were made while gradually increasing concentration of gentamicin sulfate, ranging from 2.5 × 10−8 to 4.0 × 10−5 mol L−1. The results were then compared to the measurements obtained in absence of the aminoglycoside. As the concentration of gentamycin increased, ζ-potential of AgNPs/N-GQDs gradually changed from more negative to less negative values, eventually reaching a positive surface charge when the analyte concentration exceeded 1.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 (as shown in Table 3). These findings suggest an interaction between the analyte and the hybrid nanoparticle system, which could account for the observed changes in photoluminescence.

The LSPR band of the AgNPs/N-GQDs systems were recorded in presence of gentamicin from 0.25 × 10−6 to 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 (concentration range found previously to affect the photoluminescence of AgNPs/N-GQDs). As concentration of gentamicin was increased, the LSPR spectrum intensity decreased with no noticeable band maximum spectral shifting.

The AgNPs/N-GQDs system made from AgNPs with 20 nm diammeter exhibited a better proportionally signal changing in response to the varying of concentration of gentamycin than the system containing AgNPs with a diameter of 15 nm, in this case exhibiting a sudden decrease in signal intensity as the analyte concentration increased from lower to higher levels. Such an abrupt change has also been noticed in terms of luminescence (as seen in Fig. 4). Additionally, there was a noticeable AgNPs aggregation as the LSPR band decreased as concentration of analyte increased ( Fig. 5).

The interaction between AgNPs/N-GQDs and gentamicin was evaluated by measuring photoluminescence at different temperatures (from 20 °C to 45 °C). Two different concentrations of gentamycin (1.5 × 10−7 mol L−1 and 1.5 × 10−5 mol L−1) were used, and results shown that the (L − L0)/L0 (normalized photoluminescence) decreased as temperature was increased. Such a behavior can be explained by the increased thermal energy of the species, which made it more difficult for gentamycin to interact with the AgNPs/N-GQDs system. The result obtained from the photoluminescence measurements provides evidence that gentamycin and AgNPs/N-GQDs interact forming a long-term product.

Based on the results the quenching mechanism appears be of static nature, occurring due to the formation of a ground-state complex between the AgNPs and the N-GQDs. The interaction between the sulfur and/or nitrogen functionalization on the N-GQDs and the surface of the AgNPs facilitates this complexation. Evidence for static quenching is supported by the observed changes in the photoluminescence spectra at different temperatures and due to the stability of the hybrid system in the absence of gentamicin. Photoluminescence of the N-GQDs is quenched by AgNPs through static interactions driven by chemical interaction of Ag and N-containing substituents in N-GQDs affecting excitonic recombination in N-GQDs. The extent of quenching increases with the concentration of AgNPs as energy transfer or electron transfer increases between the excited-state N-GQDs and AgNPs. The introduction of gentamicin disrupts the AgNPs/N-GQDs system interacting with AgNPs, leading to luminescence enhancement This luminescence increase further supports a static quenching mechanisms (see Fig S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing AgNPs/N-GQDs probe conditions for gentamycin determination
ce:section.ce:para: The response of the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe towards gentamycin was fine-tuned by adjusting the relative proportion of AgNPs and N-GQDs. Ten probe compositions were studied (Table S1), all of them exclusively using AgNPs of 20 nm. The amounts of N-GQDs (in total carbon content) and AgNPs were adjusted to achieve the final composition of the analytical probe.

When utilizing 2.0 mL of the N-GQDs working dispersion, with a TCC of 0.006 mg L−1, photoluminescence was not altered by gentamycin, within the 10−7 to 10−5 mol L−1 concentration range, regardless of the final concentration of AgNPs used. However, when using 7.0 mL of the N-GQDs working dispersion (0.021 mg L−1 TCC), gentamycin had an impact on the photoluminescence even when the AgNPs concentration was at its minimum value (4.5 × 10−11 mol L−1). This resulted in the photoluminescence enhancement and spectral shifting as the analyte concentration increased. Nonetheless, the sensitivity of the response was inadequate to differentiate signal variation at lower analyte concentrations. When the concentrations of AgNPs in the probe were increased to 1.2 × 10−10 mol L−1 and 2.0 × 10−10 mol L−1, a more harmonic variability in photoluminescence intensity in the presence of gentamicin occurred. Luminescence quenching and a blue shiftting of the band were initially generated at low concentrations, but the signal suffers a sudden enhancement at higher concentrations of gentamycin, affecting proportionality of response.

Finally, regarding dispersions comprising 4.0 mL of the N-GQDs working dispersion (TCC of 0.012 mg L−1), there was a positive correlation between the rise in photoluminescence and the blue shift of the spectrum as gentamicin concentration was increased. However, it was not feasible to differentiate between the lowest concentration of gentamicin (5.0 × 10−8 mol L−1) and blank (probe without gentamicin) for probes containing concentrations of AgNPs of 1.2 × 10−10 mol L−1 or 3.0 × 10−10 mol L−1.

Probes containing either 1.8 × 10−10 mol L−1 or 2.4 × 10−10 mol L−1 of AgNPs presented responses that enabled differentiation between lower concentration levels of gentamycin. The concentration of 1.8 × 10−10 mol L−1 (or 3.0 mL of the AgNPs working dispersion) was chosen over the larger one (2.4 × 10−10 mol L−1) to produce the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe.

In terms of morphology, the mean AgNPs/N-GQDs nanoparticle was 28.9 ± 8.9 nm as estimated by STEM ( Fig. 6A). Image processing analysis revealed that the aspect ratio was 1.20 ± 0.20 (as depicted in Fig. 6B), while the circularity was 0.80 ± 0.10 (as illustrated in Fig. 6C).

The stability of the AgNPs/N-GQDs as well as their interaction with aminoglycosides, are influenced by the pH. The synthesized AgNP dispersion had an original pH of 7.9 ± 0.2. Conversely, the diluted N-GQDs dispersion exhibited an original pH of 6.5 ± 0.2. To define the pH of the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe, 4.00 mL of the N-GQDs probe was mixed with 3.00 mL of AgNPs (estimated concentration of 2.4 × 10−10 mol L−1 of 20 nm nanoparticles). Water was added leading to a final 10.00 mL volume with the resulting pH 7.8. Various aqueous systems were prepared to assess the interaction between AgNPs/N-GQDs and gentamycin. These systems contained the analyte at a concentration of 3.5 × 10−6 mol L−1, and the final pH was adjusted to between 3 and 9 by adding aliquots of NaOH (0.01 mol L−1) or HCl (0.01 mol L−1).

Gentamycin sulfate has pKa values ranging from 6.2 to 9.9 and exhibited greater efficacy in interacting with the AgNPs/N-GQDs when the pH of the system was within 6.5 and 7.5, as shown in Fig. S2 that illustrates the considerable influence of pH on how analyte affects photoluminescence. It is crucial to emphasize that adding gentamycin sulfate to achieve concentrations within the range of 10−8 to 10−5 mol L−1 does not result in a pH change of more than 0.2 units in the dispersion. Therefore, the pH of the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe dispersion was adjusted to about 7.0.

The photoluminescence stability of the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe dispersion was evaluated right after preparation (conditions in Table 4). As a result of the presence of AgNPs (hydrodynamic diameter of 20 nm and final estimated concentration of 2.4 × 10−10 mol L−1), the intensity of photoluminescence reduced significantly, reaching a lower intensity that was only 30 % of the original measured from an equivalent N-GQDs dispersion without the addition of AgNPs. The quenched photoluminescence remained stable (random signal variation of 3.2 %, for up to 120 min).

The stability of the signal measured from the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe was also evaluated after mixing gentamycin, with measurements made in intervals of 5 min. The addition of gentamycin sulfate (at 5.0 × 10−5 mol L−1) resulted in partial photoluminescence restoration (about 80 % of the one measured from an equivalent N-GQDs dispersion without the addition of AgNPs), remaining stable 5 min after analyte mixing up to 120 min later (random variation of 4.3 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical figures of merit in gentamycin determination
ce:section.ce:para: Once appropriate conditions were established to induce a significant photoluminescence enhancement from the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe dispersion by gentamycin (Table 4), analytical curves were made to obtain figures of merit. As illustrated in Fig. S3A, the photoluminescence of the probe increased gradually with the increasing concentrations of gentamycin with linear response exhibiting coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.997, covering two orders of magnitude up to 6.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 (analyte concentration in the probe). The blue shifting of the emission band was observed from 435 nm (in absence of the analyte) to 425 nm (at the highest gentamicin concentration). The normalized equation for the analytical curve (Fig. S3B) was (Ln − L0)/L0 = 1.3 × 106 L mol−1 × [Gentamycin] + 0.0104. The limit of quantification (LOQ) was 3.0 × 10−7 mol L−1 based on the lowest concentration that can be experimentally differentiated from blank values (see analytical curve provided as supplementary material). From LOQ, the limit of detection (LOD was obtained as 9.1 × 10−8 mol L−1.

Precision was assessed by taking measurements from two sets of ten independent AgNPs/N-GQDs dispersion probes (excitation at 325 nm) each one containing a specific concentration of gentamycin. Precision was computed as the coefficient of variation based on the standard deviations s L and s L0. For gentamycin at 9.0 × 10−8 mol L−1, the intermediate precision was 2.6 %, at 3.0 × 10−7 mol L−1, was 1.8 %, while for gentamycin at 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 it was 1.8 %. Ten measurements of a single probe dispersion were made with gentamycin at 9.0 × 10−8 mol L−1, 3.0 × 10−7 and at 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1, in order to assess instrumental precision, with results not surpassing 2.3 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity using solid-phase extraction with aminoglycoside selective molecularly imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of the AgNPs/N-GQDs probe in detecting gentamycin in pharmaceutical samples was evaluated by mixing other substances commonly found in such compositions along with gentamycin sulfate. These concomitant substances were used at relatively higher concentration (5.0 × 10−4 mol L−1). The effect in the AgNPs/N-GQDs dispersion probe luminescence induced by these substances (excipients) were expressed in relative percent taken as a reference to the original signal of the AgNPs/N-GQDs dispersion probe. Sodium metabisulfite, methylparaben, and propylparaben did not significantly affect the AgNPs/N-GQDs dispersion probe, as indicated by a signal variation under 3 %. Anhydrous disodium EDTA caused an increase of around 17 % in the measured photoluminescence, although it was less effective than the change caused by gentamicin sulfate, which produced a comparable signal variation at a concentration 100 times lower (1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1).

A SPE procedure was employed using a MIP-AMG and the recovery of gentamycin was evaluated by loading a control solution containing the analyte (at 1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1) into the cartridge. After elution with aqueous HCl solution (pH 3.5), the eluate was brought to a pH of roughly 7.0 using small additions of a 0.01 mol L−1 NaOH solution before including it into the AgNPs/N-GQDs dispersion probe. The signal variation imposed by the eluted solution was only 2.5 ± 0.5 % less intense than the signal produced by an equivalent gentamycin standard that did not undergo the SPE procedure. This suggests that the proposed method can effectively retain and then recover gentamycin from the solid-phase, enabling percent recovery of at least 97.5 %.

Two simulated samples were created to assess efficacy of the SPE with concentration of gentamycin sulfate in both at 5.0 × 10−4 mol L−1. The first sample consisted solely of the analyte in water, while the second sample contained the analyte along with excipients (each excipient at a final concentration of 5.0 × 10−2 mol L−1). After applying the SPE procedure to the simulated samples, and adjusting the pH of the eluted analyte, a certain volume was mixed in the probe reaching a final concentration of 1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1 for gentamycin and 1.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 for interferents. The SPE approach was effective with percent recoveries of (97.0 ± 0.5) % for the sample without excipients and (96.4 ± 0.9) % for the sample with excipients. The percent recoveries were calculated based on the response obtained from a gentamycin standard of 1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1 that had not undergone the SPE approach.

The AgNPs/N-GQDs dispersion probe was utilized to determine gentamycin in two veterinary pharmaceutical samples previously treated using the SPE procedure. The method detects the total gentamycin content and is not specific to any particular one. In contrast, the HPLC method, used to compare results, includes a chemical derivatization with OPA resulting to chromatographic peaks for each of the various gentamycins. Therefore, the chromatographic determination involved the total peak area of all the detected gentamycins.

It is important to indicate that detected analyte values using the N-GQDs probe is orders of magnitude lower than the gentamycin content typical in pharmaceutical formulations. This fact allows analyst to adjust dilution factor of sample (or adjust volume of sample added to the probe) allowing defining a specific order of magnitude of concentration for operation. Three replicates of each pharmaceutical sample and a simulated (controlled) sample were analyzed in three different days with results compared using two-tailed t-student test and analysis of variance (single-factor ANOVA) both at 95 % confidence limit for n1 = n2 = 3. The analysis of the simulated sample yielded recovery of 98.0 ± 0.5 %, which was calculated based on the expected concentration of analyte. The recoveries for the veterinary pharmaceuticals using both methods were above 100 % (as shown in Table 5), indicating that these medicines probably contained gentamycin higher than those indicated in the pharmaceutical formulation instructions. This fact was confirmed by HPLC analysis, which also produced recoveries above 100 % and close to the ones obtained using the proposed method.

Statistical analysis indicated no significant distinction between outcomes obtained from the proposed method and the chromatographic method for both samples. The experimental Student t-values for formulations A and B were 1.74 and 1.86, respectively, whereas the critical Student t-value is 2.44. The pharmaceutical formulations' results underwent a single-factor ANOVA analysis and the resulting experimental F-value was 3, lower than the critical F-value of 5.98. Therefore, it was concluded that the method produce accurate results.

AGREE is a tool that considers the 12 principles of green analytical chemistry (GAC) determine the greenness of an analytical method [30], considering waste generation, reagent toxicity, energy usage, miniaturization, and automation among other aspects. AGREE calculator generates a final score on a scale from 0 to 1 which is placed at the center of a pictogram, surrounded by 12 segments (each one corresponding to one of the GAC principles). The color code varies from red to dark green as a specific segment, or the overall score, becomes more environmentally friendly.

The proposed analytical method was scored 0.63 (Fig. S4), indicating advantages in half of the GAC principles (4, 6, 7, 9, 11, and 12). Principle 1 has an acceptable score as the method is off-line. Principle 2 received an acceptable score as the analysis requires pre-treatment using SPE, which consumes additional sample volume, however, the sample volumes added to the AgNPs/N-GQDs probes are in the microliter range, minimizing reagent consumption. Principle 3, received a low score, since the method meets the requirements for off-line measurements. The use of the AgNPs/N-GQDs positively impacts Principle 4 as reduced amounts of reagents are used to prepare the probe, and the sample is treated with a simple procedure. Principle 5 is of low score as the method is manual, although it could be semi-automated if determination is adapted to FIA, and the method is not miniaturized. There is no use of derivatization procedures, fully complying with Principle 6. Although batch analysis still requires 3 mL of the diluted probe, dispersion and sample volumes are in microliter volumes, producing a relatively low and diluted waste (Principle 7). The method only focuses on one analyte but may target total content of aminoglycoside in a sample as the SPE cartridge solid phase is very selective towards aminoglycosides (Principle 8). Principle 9 is attended to as the energy demanded by the method is minimal. Principle 10 is also favorably addressed as part of reagents to produce probes require citric acid and glutathione (organic, simple molecules readily available). Finally, Principles 11 and 12 received a high score due to the use of low-toxicity reagents, such as citric acid as a precursor for GQDs, along with ensured operational safety.

The effect of AgNPs of different sizes were tested for photoluminescence quenching of N-GQDs dispersion and to produce a hybrid nanostructure (AgNPs/N-GQDs). The results showed that adding gentamycin to the AgNPs/N-GQDs dispersion tend to restore the original photoluminescence of N-GQDs in a turn-on effect. Moreover, a distinct trend was observed between systems containing AgNPs of different sizes. Spectral shift was observed along with quenching when larger AgNPs (20–36 nm) were associated with N-GQDs, suggesting a change in the bandgap of the quantum dots. Based on the study, it was found that AgNPs, with a hydrodynamic size of 20 nm, were the most effective in detecting gentamycin. Experimental parameters were established ensuring stable and consistent analytical response with LOD of 9.0 × 10−8 mol L−1 and a linear range up to 6.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. The method was successfully used to analyze two veterinary pharmaceuticals, demonstrating selectivity of the SPE procedure. Results aligned with those obtained using the HPLC method (with chemical derivatization). Importantly, the method developed is simple and cost-effective, reaching a 0.63 score in a greenness calculator (AGREE).
